name: Sweetfern
summary: Nitrogen-fixing acid-pioneer shrub for dry disturbed openings — road cuts, old logging landings, ridge tops, burned-over areas. Strongly aromatic fern-like leaves that smell of bayberry-mint when crushed. Builds soil on degraded ground.
notes: Native throughout the Poconos and northern PA. Hosts ~49 Lepidoptera species including the spectacular Sweetfern Underwing and Gray Hairstreak. Nitrogen-fixing via Frankia bacteria symbiosis (actinorhizal). Notoriously difficult to transplant — buy small container stock (NOT bare-root), plant in spring, mulch heavily, accept 2x mortality and plant accordingly.

planting.aspect: Dry openings; ridge tops; old logging landings; disturbed acid soils
planting.soil: Strongly acidic; sandy or gravelly; well-drained
planting.site_notes: BUY SMALL CONTAINER STOCK — bare-root transplants almost always fail. Plant in spring, mulch deeply with wood chips, expect ~50% mortality and plant 2x what you need. No deer protection needed. Excellent for skid trails and old log landings.
